content.full_text:
April 24, 1981


Charles C. Chatfield
Chatfield Investment Company
7700 College Town Dr., Suite 204
Sacramento, CA 95826

Re: Your Request for Written Advice

Dear Mr. Chatfield:

Reference is made to our letter to you dated April 3, 1981, wherein we gave you interim advice regarding your participation in decisions of the California Horse Racing Board (CHRB) affecting Mr. Lloyd Arnold. As we indicated to you, before we could give you permanent written advice, the matter would need to be submitted to the Commission for its consideration due to the unique facts involved. We asked whether you desired to remain on the Board and would be in need of such further advice.

Based upon our conversation of April 21, I am advised that you have elected to remain on the CHRB, but that you do not desire us to pursue the matter further since you have elected to disqualify yourself from participating in any CHRB decision affecting Mr. Lloyd Arnold. On that basis, there is no continuing need for our interim advice; accordingly, it is hereby withdrawn. Should you at some future date desire permanent written advice about participation in any specific decision involving Mr. Arnold, or any other person for that matter, please feel free to so request.

Your self-imposed disqualification is both commendable and problematical. So long as you correctly ascertain those CHRB decisions in which you should not participate, you will avoid liability under the Political Reform Act. Of course, since you are not receiving formal advice from the Commission, you also have no immunity. And, we would point out that under the Act, disqualification from making or participating

Charles C. Chatfield
April 24, 1981
Page Two


in the making of a decision is required whenever such a
decision foreseeably will have a material financial effect
on a financial interest, regardless of whether that effect
is direct or indirect. For your guidance in this matter, I
enclose copies of relevant portions of the Commission's
regulations and the Political Reform Act.

Again, should you have any further questions or need
our assistance in the future, please contact us at
(916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,


Robert F. Leidigh
Counsel
Legal Division

REL:kp

Enc.: Government Code Sections 87100, 87101, 87103
2 Cal. Adm. Code Sections 18700, 18701, 18702, 18703
Political Reform Act

Chatfield
Investment
Company
REAL ESTATE
INVESTMENTS

March 24, 1981

Mr. Ted Prim
Fair Political Practice Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, California 98514

Dear Mr. Prim:

This letter is written to ask for an opinion from your office as to whether I may or may not continue to serve on the California Horse Racing Commission. The main question revolves around a potential conflict of interest, the facts of which are as follows:

I am in the Real Estate investment business and have been so for 15 years. I specialize in buying investment properties for myself and a select list of clients. During the month of January I was contacted by Mr. Lloyd Arnold who wanted me to look for a Real Estate investment opportunity for a company in which he is a stockholder, namely the Illini Beef Company of Illinois. The Illini Beef Company is a public held company located in Geneseo, Illinois. Their stock is traded over the counter and they have been in business for a number of years. Mr. Arnold informed me that he was a stockholder, but did not control the company nor was he on the Board of Directors.

During the month of February an investment came to light that I have wanted to purchase for quite some time, namely 670 acres located in Yolo County. I contacted Mr. Gus Lohman, the Chairman of the Board of Illini Beef and appraised him of the investment and he agreed to come to Sacramento to look at it to see if his company would have some interest. Mr. Lohman flew to Sacramento and, along with Mr. Arnold and myself, reviewed the investment.

After Mr. Lohman's visit, I felt that his company was not interested, so I proceeded to raise the money needed here locally, with my own investors. Some time during that week, Mr. Lohman called me and said his Board would be interested if I would put up half of the money needed to purchase the property. I told him that that would not be a problem and he then said his Board would meet and he would let me know of their interest. Within that week Mr. Lohman again phoned me and made an appointment for his Board to fly to Sacramento to review the property on site. They did so the following Monday and after a day-long meeting, they proceeded to have a closed Board

7700 COLLEGE TOWN DRIVE, SUITE 204, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95826 (916) 383-8090

Mr. Ted Prim                                                    March 24, 1981
Page 2


Meeting and voted to pick up half of the investment. We have formed a Joint
Venture between Illini Beef and South Port Investors, a California Limited
Partnership, of which I am the General Partner, and purchased the property.

My question is this: because of Lloyd Arnold's involvement as a stock-
holder in Illini Beef Company and also being licensed by the State of California
to run Harness Meets at various State tracks which are regulated by the
California Horse Racing Commission, of which I am a member, do I face a potential
conflict of interest?

I have talked to my own attorney and to the Attorney General's office and
both feel that this is not in any way a conflict of interest. It is also my
understanding that Mr. Fuller of the Attorney General's office has spoken with
you about the same. I would like your opinion as quickly as possible so I
may know what I must do.

I should also point out that I have resigned from the Commission, but the
Governor refuses to accept my resignation. I have told him nothing of this
matter and will not do so until I hear from you.

Many thanks for your consideration.


                                                            Sincerely,

                                                            [signature]
                                                            CHAS. C. CHATFIELD

CCC/mlnk

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Telephone • • • Administration • • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5660        322-5660           322-5601           322-6461           322-5444

April 3, 1981


Charles C. Chatfield
Chatfield Investment Company
7700 College Town Dr., Ste. 204
Sacramento, CA  95826

Re: Your Request for Written
Advice

Dear Mr. Chatfield:

This letter will confirm our telephone conversation of
yesterday afternoon, April 2, 1981. Because your situation
raises a unique question of interpretation under the Political
Reform Act, the staff believes this matter should be presented
to the full Fair Political Practices Commission for a ruling
before we can give you permanent written advice pursuant to
Government Code Section 83114. In the interim, you need not
disqualify yourself under the Act from participating in
decisions affecting Mr. Lloyd Arnold and his racing interests.
Please advise us as soon as practicable whether or not you
desire further consideration of this matter.

Let me again state to you my appreciation for your
cooperation, candor and approach in this matter. It has
been a pleasure to serve you and should you have further
questions, please do not hesitate to call upon us.

Sincerely,

Robert E. Ledigh
Counsel
Legal Division

REL:plh

Tentative land pact aids port

Agreement could open 1,000 acres to commercial and industrial uses

By Max Miller
Bee Staff Writer

An "agreement in principle" between the Port of Sacramento and a partnership of investors is being hailed as a move that could open 1,000 acres of land to industrial, commercial and other uses.

The move is expected to benefit both the port and the new city of West Sacramento.

The Sacramento-Yolo Port Commission has approved preliminary negotiations with Southport Investors, a limited partnership headed by banker-developer Frank C. Ramos and investor Lloyd Arnold, for several seasons the promoter of harness racing at Cal Expo.

Southport Investors owns 700 acres of land stretching for 1½ miles along the east bank of the Sacramento Deep Water Channel immediately south of the harbor in West Sacramento.

To improve access to the Southport property, the Port Commission has preliminarily agreed to

grant easements across port-owned land in exchange for Southport's commitment to build levees and provide areas to deposit dredge material from the first phase of a project to deepen the 40-mile-long ship channel to 35 feet.

Port Director Dennis Clark said the arrangement would save the port "considerable money" in the dredging of levees and the acquisition of an estimated 200 to 250 acres of land to store material dredged from the ship channel.

The port owns 400 acres of land west of the Arlington Heights subdivision and the ship channel on the south bank of the harbor.

Clark said the agreement with Southport is the first in a series of moves to implement West Sacramento's general plan calling for a bridge linking commercial and industrial facilities on the north side of the port and ship channel with the Southport area.

Plans under study call for a bridge across the ship channel at the foot of Industrial Boulevard, about 1½ miles east of the present bridge, which

crosses the barge canal on Jefferson Boulevard.

Another proposal calls for use of port property to locate a 2 million-gallon water tank for the city of West Sacramento.

West Sacramento City Manager Gene Roh said the tank will be one of three to be constructed to improve the city's water treatment system.

"The port really has worked with us very well," Roh said. "We have been trying to keep these tanks out of residential areas.

"Without question we are very supportive of the idea of the bridge poing in and we are exploring the possibility that it could be a part of the city's redevelopment project."

In addition to the preliminary arrangement with Southport Investors, the Port Commission approved the lease of 5,000 square feet of space in the port's World Trade Center building to house West Sacramento city offices.

Officials of Southport Investors were not available Friday for comment on the negotiations with the Port Commission.

are not the way to open up a conversation. I hate to see a response to people who commit such acts or condoned such acts."

Earlier in the morning, it appeared that ARDAC's prediction of a "major demonstration" at the UCD Primate Center was a bust.

By 9 a.m., about 20 protesters had assembled at the center, about three miles west of the main campus, where they marched and chanted

days before appeared to have been there for about six months and that the skeletal remains appeared to be intact.

Police said previously that because the bodies are so badly decomposed, they were not able to determine the sex. The four other identified victims linked to accused murderer Morris Solomon Jr. were young women.

sections.question: My question is this: because of Lloyd Arnold's involvement as a stockholder in Illini Beef Company and also being licensed by the State of California to run Harness Meets at various State tracks which are regulated by the California Horse Racing Commission, of which I am a member, do I face a potential conflict of interest?
sections.question_synthetic: The writer asks whether serving on the California Horse Racing Commission while engaged in a real estate joint venture with Illini Beef Company (a company in which Lloyd Arnold is a stockholder and who is also licensed to run harness meets at state tracks) creates a conflict of interest.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter to FPPC, not a response. The document contains the question/inquiry from the requester. No FPPC response or conclusion is included in this document. The document also contains unrelated newspaper clippings appended at the end which do not pertain to the FPPC inquiry. Confidence reduced slightly due to document organization and appended unrelated materials.

embedding.summary: This is an incoming request to the FPPC from a California Horse Racing Commission member asking whether his participation in a real estate investment joint venture with Illini Beef Company, whose stockholder Lloyd Arnold operates harness racing meets at state-regulated tracks, creates a conflict of interest.
